message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    To estimate vaccine effectiveness (VE) of COVID-19 mRNA vaccines administered during pregnancy. Primary (2-dose) vaccination was compared with unvaccinated women; and 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    booster (3rd dose) with primary (2-dose) vaccination.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Target trial emulation cohort study.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Routinely collected health data from UK, Sweden, Spain, and Norway. All had complete vaccine exposure data and were mapped to the OMOP common data model.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Pregnant women (aged 12-55) with ≤34 weeks of gestation, no history of COVID-19 in the 90 days prior and ≥1 year of data visibility. Women were matched 1:1 to those with no dose and 2-dose vaccination, respectively, using weekly sequential matching from vaccination roll-out to 2023 (Swedish data: 2024).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main outcome measures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The main outcomes were COVID-19 infection, hospitalisation and Intensive Care Unit (ICU) admission (COVID-related, excluding delivery-related care). Hazard ratios (HR) were estimated with Cox regression from 15 days post-vaccination, and VE was reported as 100 x (1-HR). Analyses were stratified by pregnancy trimester and vaccine brand. Database-specific results were pooled using a random-effects meta-analysis.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We included 62,061 and 41,096 pregnant women at date of vaccination with a first and third dose, respectively. Median follow-up across databases ranged from 42 to 55 days for primary vaccination and 73 to 116 days for boosters. For primary vaccination, the meta-analysis showed a VE of 26.2% (16.2 to 35.1; I
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    =0.7) against infection and 55.6% (44.1 to 64.8; I
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    =0) against hospitalisation. For boosters, corresponding figures were 33.6% (-1.6 to 56.6; I
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    =1) and 34.1% (0.6 to 56.4; I
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    =0.5). Event counts were insufficient to analyse VE against ICU admission. Results were consistent across pregnancy trimesters and vaccine brands.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Vaccination with a primary (2-dose) or a 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    booster (3rd dose) during pregnancy was effective in preventing COVID-19 hospitalisation, with modest and heterogeneous VE against infection across 4 European countries.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Summary</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>What is already known on this topic</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="simple">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>- Pregnant women are at increased risk of severe complications from COVID-19, which can affect both mothers and their infants.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>- Vaccination during pregnancy has been shown to be safe and effective, with benefits of vaccination outweighing any potential risks.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>- Studies examining the effectiveness of COVID-19 vaccines during pregnancy have primarily focused on primary vaccination with BNT162b2, with limited follow-up, sample size and representativeness.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>What this study adds</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="simple">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>- This study provides evidence on the effectiveness of COVID-19 mRNA vaccines administered during pregnancy using routinely collected data of over 124,122 pregnancies from 4 European countries.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>- Primary (2-dose) vaccination against COVID-19 during pregnancy was effective in preventing infection during pregnancy with a vaccine effectiveness of 26%, and 55% for preventing COVID-19 related hospitalisations.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>
                          - A 1
                          <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                          booster (3
                          <jats:sup>rd</jats:sup>
                          dose) had a vaccine effectiveness of 30-35% against COVID-19 infection and COVID-19-related hospitalisation compared to primary (2-dose) vaccination.
                        </jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: Effectiveness of COVID-19 mRNA primary and booster vaccination against infection and hospitalisation during pregnancy: a target trial emulation and meta-analysis of data from 4 European countries
